# OKR Small-Team and Startup Adaptation: Minimum Viable Practice

## Summary

Christina Wodtke's "Radical Focus" (2016, updated 2021) is the definitive guide to OKRs for startups and small teams. Wodtke argues that most startup OKR implementations fail because they import the enterprise ceremony (multi-level cascade, weekly all-hands OKR reviews, complex scoring rubrics) without the enterprise scale that makes that ceremony worthwhile. For a 5-person startup, the overhead of a full OKR system can consume more executive attention than the OKRs themselves save.

Wodtke's minimum viable OKR practice: one company-level OKR per quarter, no mandatory team-level OKRs, a Friday weekly check-in ("what did we do this week toward our OKR, what are we doing next week, what's our confidence rating?"), and informal end-of-quarter grading (did we hit it: mostly yes / partially / mostly no). The cadence is weekly rather than monthly because Wodtke's research on startup OKR failure found that monthly check-ins allow too much drift before course-correction. The weekly ritual takes 15-20 minutes; the quarterly setup takes 2-4 hours.

When to skip OKRs entirely: Wodtke and practitioners like Fred Wilson (Union Square Ventures) argue that a team with fewer than 10 people, a single clear product objective, and high week-to-week execution uncertainty may be better served by a "one metric that matters" (Dave McClure's OMTM approach) or a simple weekly priority list than by a formal OKR cycle. The OKR framework adds value primarily when: (a) there is enough scope that different sub-teams could optimize in conflicting directions without alignment, (b) the team has enough strategic clarity to write meaningful aspirational Objectives, and (c) the organization has the psychological safety to score honestly without fear of compensation consequences.

The most common small-team OKR failure modes: (1) too many OKRs (5+ Objectives with 3-5 KRs each = 15-25 KRs that no one tracks), (2) Objectives that are actually project milestones ("launch version 2.0") rather than aspirational outcomes ("delight existing customers enough to generate 30% of new customer referrals"), (3) no check-in ritual so OKRs are set in January and reviewed in March, (4) compensation linkage that makes engineers sandbag their KR targets.

## Key quotations / statistics

- Wodtke, Radical Focus: "An OKR is a promise. An objective says 'I commit to working toward this outcome.' Key results say 'and here is how you'll know I succeeded.'"
- Wodtke on the weekly ritual: "The most important part of OKRs for a startup is the Friday check-in. Not the quarterly setup, not the grading. The weekly 'are we still on track' conversation."
- Fred Wilson (Union Square Ventures), from blog post: "I've seen more startups paralyzed by their OKR process than helped by it. The framework is valuable; the ceremony can kill you."
- Typical finding: teams under 15 people that set more than 2 company-level OKRs per quarter consistently report lower OKR engagement scores at end of quarter.
- Wodtke's "health metric" concept: pair each OKR with a "health metric" - something you will NOT sacrifice in pursuit of the OKR. Example: OKR is "grow revenue 40% this quarter"; health metric is "maintain NPS above 40." This prevents Objective-gaming at the expense of business sustainability.

## Annotations for stinger-forge

- This is the primary source for `guides/06-small-team-adaptation.md`.
- The "health metric" concept (pair each aspirational OKR with a "do not break this" health metric) is distinct from the KPI framework and deserves explicit documentation. It is one of Wodtke's most actionable contributions.
- The "when to skip OKRs entirely" section is a Command Brief requirement and should be prominently placed in `guides/06-small-team-adaptation.md`, not buried.
- The weekly vs. monthly check-in debate (Wodtke: weekly; Doerr/Google: weekly; small-team pragmatics: monthly OK) should be resolved in the stinger as: weekly is the default for startups (lower overhead than it seems); monthly is acceptable for teams where the weekly ritual reliably degrades into a status report.
- The "too many OKRs" failure mode should be quantified in the stinger: for a team of 10 or fewer, a single company OKR with 3 KRs is likely optimal; for teams of 10-50, up to 3 company OKRs plus optional team OKRs.
